Measuring Congress in the AI Era:
What Modern Vote Prediction Reveals About
Members, Bills, and Institutions

Andrew B. Hall*

Draft: July 2026 — preliminary, comments welcome

Abstract
Nearly everything political science measures about members of Congress—ideology, loyalty, issue positions—is extracted from models that predict rollcall votes. Prediction quality therefore bounds measurement quality in an informational sense: structure a model cannot predict is structure no measure derived from it can contain—though, as this paper’s final section shows, the converse does not hold, and separating predictive signal from construct-valid measurement is itself part of the work. We build the strongest vote predictor on our benchmark—an ensemble reading rollcall-level text with modern language-model encoders, anchored on member histories, with calibrated probabilities (86.2% accuracy on strictly future votes; 85.1% on contested votes; its frozen, hash-pinned predecessor scored 85.7% on the 9,451 votes Congress cast in the three weeks after that artifact was finalized, and the ensemble’s own prospective ledger is running)—and then use it as a measurement instrument. The model’s internals recover and extend the classical measurement canon: positions correlating at r = .98 with DW-NOMINATE, party-loyalty residuals that identify the chamber’s mavericks by name, issue-specific positions that recover libertarian non-interventionism from votes alone, bill-level cutpoints locating each rollcall on the ideological scale, and a new measure of out-of-character votes. Just as informative is what the instrument cannot do. Amendment coalitions resist text-based prediction because they form around deals rather than content—a measurable boundary on what legislative language reveals. And no model survives a

*Stanford University. Replication materials, including the full pipeline, model registry, pre-registered experiment ledger, and session logs of the AI-assisted development process, are available in the project repository. Every statistic and figure in this paper is generated programmatically from the analysis code’s output files; no number is transcribed by hand.

<!-- PAGE BREAK -->

# Measuring Congress in the AI Era: What Modern Vote Prediction Reveals About Members, Bills, and Institutions

Andrew B. Hall*

Draft: July 2026 — preliminary, comments welcome

## Abstract

Nearly everything political science measures about members of Congress—ideology, loyalty, issue positions—is extracted from models that predict rollcall votes. Prediction quality therefore bounds measurement quality in an informational sense: structure a model cannot predict is structure no measure derived from it can contain—though, as this paper's final section shows, the converse does not hold, and separating predictive signal from construct-valid measurement is itself part of the work. We build the strongest vote predictor on our benchmark—an ensemble reading rollcall-level text with modern language-model encoders, anchored on member histories, with calibrated probabilities (86.2% accuracy on strictly future votes; 85.1% on contested votes; its frozen, hash-pinned predecessor scored 85.7% on the 9,451 votes Congress cast in the three weeks after that artifact was finalized, and the ensemble's own prospective ledger is running)—and then use it as a measurement instrument. The model's internals recover and extend the classical measurement canon: positions correlating at $r = .98$ with DW-NOMINATE, party-loyalty residuals that identify the chamber's mavericks by name, issue-specific positions that recover libertarian non-interventionism from votes alone, bill-level cutpoints locating each rollcall on the ideological scale, and a new measure of out-of-character votes. Just as informative is what the instrument cannot do. Amendment coalitions resist text-based prediction because they form around deals rather than content—a measurable boundary on what legislative language reveals. And no model survives a

*Stanford University. Replication materials, including the full pipeline, model registry, pre-registered experiment ledger, and session logs of the AI-assisted development process, are available in the project repository. Every statistic and figure in this paper is generated programmatically from the analysis code's output files; no number is transcribed by hand.

<!-- PAGE BREAK -->

change in majority control: member histories actively mislead across the 2022 House flip, showing that much of what vote models measure is institutional role, not stable preference. Career-spanning NOMINATE scores, by contrast, nearly match the deep model’s cross-congress accuracy—a genuine vindication of traditional measurement design. We release the benchmark, models, and a pre-registered ledger of all results, positive and negative.

## 1 Introduction

The empirical study of Congress rests on measurement, and the measurement rests on prediction. An ideal point is the parameter of a model built to predict votes *(Poole and Rosenthal, 1997; Clinton, Jackman and Rivers, 2004)*; a party-unity score is a prediction from party membership; an interest-group rating is a prediction from a hand-picked vote subset. The field’s measures of legislators are, almost without exception, the interpretable residue of vote-prediction machinery. This has a bracing implication that the measurement literature rarely states: how well the machinery predicts is an upper bound on how much the measures can know. Structure a model fails to predict is structure no measure derived from it can contain.

For four decades the machinery in question has been the spatial voting model, and it earned its dominance: one or two dimensions classify around ninety percent of votes in the modern Congress. The past fifteen years of scaling research has pushed on the machinery from every side: legislator-specific unpredictability *(Lauderdale, 2010)*, issue-specific dimensions estimated with topic-weighted votes *(Lauderdale and Clark, 2014; Crespin and Rohde, 2010)*, joint models of votes and speech *(Kim, Londregan and Ratkovic, 2018)*, computational estimators for massive data *(Imai, Lo and Olmsted, 2016)*, supervised learning as a measurement bridge *(Bonica, 2018)*, bill and status-quo locations for testing theories of lawmaking *(Peress, 2013; Richman, 2011)*, and, most recently, models built to rescue the members whom one-dimensional scaling visibly mis-measures—ideological extremists who vote with the opposite pole against the middle *(Duck-Mayr and Montgomery, 2023)* and majority members who cast protest votes against bills they prefer *(Fowler and Lewis, 2026)*. A parallel literature at the intersection of machine learning and legislative studies tried to read the bills themselves *(Gerrish and Blei, 2011, 2012; Kraft, Jain and Rush, 2016)*, but worked with the text tools of its era and went quiet before modern language models existed. Today’s encoders read legislative text incomparably better, and prediction bounds measurement. Do better predictors deliver richer,

<!-- PAGE BREAK -->

sharper, more trustworthy measures of Congress? What new things can we now measure? And where does even the best modern predictor hit a wall—and what do the walls teach us about the institution?

This paper answers in three movements. *First, we build and validate the instrument* (Section 2). On an open benchmark of 10.6 million voting decisions (101st–119th Congresses), we develop an ensemble model that reads rollcall-level text with sentence encoders, maps it to vote-choice parameters through deep heads, anchors on each member’s behavioral history, and reports calibrated probabilities. On strictly future votes within a congress it reaches 86.2% accuracy (85.1% on contested votes; log loss 0.324), far beyond both the classical spatial model and the 2011–2016 text-based models, which—under proper scoring rules and honest temporal splits—never actually beat a simple table of member voting rates (Table 1). Because a measurement instrument is only as credible as its evaluation, we score every model under four regimes of increasing difficulty and add a fifth test immune to researcher iteration by construction: models frozen and hash-pinned, then scored on the 9,451 votes Congress cast in the following weeks. The frozen model’s prospective performance (0.331 log loss, 85.7% accuracy) matches its development performance, certifying that the numbers here are not artifacts of adaptive overfitting.

*Second, we measure* (Sections 3 and 4). From the instrument’s internals we extract, and validate against external benchmarks, a suite of member- and bill-level measures. Learned member positions correlate with DW-NOMINATE’s first dimension at $r=.98$ (Figure 4)—the sanity check—and then go further. A *party-loyalty residual* isolates discipline from ideology and independently rediscovers the canonical mavericks of the last decade—Walter Jones, Justin Amash, Collin Peterson, Kyrsten Sinema—from votes alone (Figure 5). *Issue-specific positions* recover, without any text about the members, the libertarian non-interventionist bloc (dovish on defense, civil-libertarian on crime, otherwise very conservative) and the hawkish wing of the Democratic party (Figure 6). At the bill level, every rollcall acquires a *cutpoint*—the location on the ideological scale where a member becomes indifferent—and the distribution of cutpoints against the distribution of members maps the strategic structure of the agenda: which policy areas divide the parties at the center, which split one party internally, which pass by consensus (Figure 9). And because the model outputs calibrated probabilities for every vote, its largest errors constitute a new dataset of *out-of-character votes*—defections the best available behavioral model could not see coming (Figure 8)—a systematic starting point for studying pressure, persuasion, and idiosyncratic representation.

*Third, we measure the walls* (Section 5). Two boundaries survived every attempt we

<!-- PAGE BREAK -->

pre-registered against them, and both are substantive findings about Congress rather than engineering shortfalls. Amendment votes carry a third of the model’s remaining error, yet supplying amendments’ actual purpose text makes prediction *worse*, not better—a result we establish with a placebo-controlled design. Within a bill, the model exploits the correlation of the amendment series more effectively than the amendments’ content: amendment coalitions form around deals, not language. That is a measured boundary on what legislative text reveals—content organizes coalitions on final passage, context organizes them on amendments. Second, when majority control of the House changed in 2022, the member-history features learned within the prior majority configuration partially inverted: pooled member records *anti-transfer* across the flip (a member who reliably supported procedural motions in the majority reliably opposes them in the minority), our best model posts higher log loss on the unseen congress than a three-way count table that merely knows who holds the chamber, and a simple logit on frozen DW-NOMINATE scores nearly matches the deep model’s transfer accuracy (62.0% versus 62.1%). The lesson is constitutional in the small: a large share of what any vote-based measure captures is institutional role, not stable preference, and measures built within one majority configuration should be carried across configurations only with care. That career-spanning NOMINATE scores—estimated from behavior in both roles—are the most durable object in our entire horse race is a quiet vindication of the traditional design.

Throughout, we practice the evaluation discipline the measurement stakes demand: every development experiment was pre-registered in a public ledger with hypothesis and falsification test written before the run, selection touched validation data only, the final model comparison observed test sets exactly once, and negative results are reported with their mechanisms. The benchmark, code, frozen artifacts, and ledger are public; a companion paper develops the member-level measures at full depth.

## 2 The Instrument: Prediction You Can Build Measures On

### 2.1 Data and task

We predict individual voting decisions: given everything observable before a rollcall, output each member’s probability of voting yea. The panel covers every yea/nay decision in the 101st through 119th Congresses from Voteview *(Lewis et al., 2026)*—10.6 million decisions—with bill-side information (titles, policy areas, dated summary versions, spon

<!-- PAGE BREAK -->

sors, amendment purposes) from government BILLSTATUS records, which begin with the 108th Congress. The scope division is strict and worth stating once: *every forecasting result in this paper uses the text-era congresses (108th–119th) only*; the pre-text congresses enter only the completion-regime spatial fits, which use no text. Rollcall-level text is assembled strictly from information available before each vote; the feature-timing audit and text-coverage accounting are in Appendix B. Splits are deterministic and content-hashed; a common harness strips labels before any model sees evaluation rows.

### 2.2 Measurement definitions

Because the paper’s contribution is measurement, the derived quantities are defined here once, precisely. The model outputs $\hat{p}_{iv}$, the probability member $i$ votes yea on rollcall $v$, from features dated strictly before $v$ (the feature-timing audit is Appendix B). The *spatial fits* estimate $P(\text{yea})=\sigma(a_{v}x_{i}+b_{v}+c_{i})$ per chamber-congress with party-sign initialization; a rollcall’s *cutpoint* is $-(b_{v}+\bar{c})/a_{v}$, reported only when $|a_{v}|\geq 0.35$ (the location is unidentified for near-valence votes), and its *direction* is $\text{sign}(a_{v})$. The *loyalty residual* is $\bar{y}_{i}^{U}-\overline{\hat{p}}_{i}^{U}$ over member $i$’s party-unity votes $U$ (majorities of the two parties opposed), where the expectation comes from the spatial fit; members need at least 100 unity votes. *Issue-specific positions* refit the spatial model within each policy area (fifty votes minimum per member-area) and are reported as deviations from the member’s all-votes position after per-area standardization. The *out-of-character score* of a realized vote $y_{iv}$ is $-\log\hat{p}_{iv}(y_{iv})$ under the frozen ensemble; Section 3.4 states its in-sample estimand explicitly. All quantities are estimated from, and therefore conditional on, the *realized rollcall agenda*: they describe behavior on the votes leaders chose to hold, a conditioning we return to throughout.

### 2.3 Why evaluation regimes are measurement questions

A model’s fitness as a measurement instrument depends on *what kind* of prediction it is good at, so we evaluate under regimes with different and progressively stricter information constraints: *completion* (random individual votes held out, same-rollcall votes observable—the psychometric setting in which ideal points are traditionally assessed); *random rollcall holdout* (whole rollcalls held out, interleaved in time—the 2011–2016 literature’s setting); *temporal forecast* (the final ten percent of each congress-chamber’s rollcalls—a true forecaster’s setting); *congress-out transfer* (train through the 116th Congress, predict the 118th, across the majority flip); and *prospective* (hash-pinned frozen models

<!-- PAGE BREAK -->

scored only on votes that occur after freezing, following the logic of the reusable holdout problem (Dwork et al., 2015)). Our primary metric is log loss—a proper scoring rule that rewards honest probabilities—because measurement consumers use the probabilities, not just the classifications; accuracy, AUC, contested-vote accuracy, and APRE are reported throughout for comparability.

Table 1 and Figure 1 summarize the audit; three points matter for everything downstream. First, completion. We report it in the field's own statistics—correct classification, APRE, and geometric mean probability, the fit vocabulary of the NOMINATE tradition (Carroll et al., 2013); log loss is  $-\log(\mathrm{GMP})$ , so our primary metric is the literature's in different units. An eight-dimensional model reaches GMP 0.858 (93.8% classification) on held-out votes against 0.849 for frozen DW-NOMINATE with estimated rollcall parameters (Appendix Table 8)—and the location of that margin is itself a finding. Figure 2 compares the two models member by member on identical held-out votes. For the typical member the classical model is nearly unimprovable (median GMP 0.858 versus 0.863; ours is higher for 67.1% of members); the large gains concentrate in precisely the members the past decade of scaling research built bespoke models to rescue—Ron Paul (GMP 0.27 → 0.61), Justin Amash, Thomas Massie, Dennis Kucinich: the ends-against-the-middle and protest voters of Duck-Mayr and Montgomery (2023) and Fowler and Lewis (2026), recovered here by one flexible model rather than phenomenon-specific likelihoods. Convergent validity—the discipline of checking a new measure against every external benchmark available (Shor and McCarty, 2011)—holds throughout: per-congress positions correlate with Nokken-Poole scores (Nokken and Poole, 2004) at  $r = 0.96 - 0.98$  across recent chamber-

<!-- PAGE BREAK -->

![img-0.jpeg](img-0.jpeg)
Figure 1: The regime audit at a glance. Moving right, less about the vote's context is observable at prediction time. The 2011-2016 architectures never beat the member-history table in any true forecasting regime; the modern gains are large within a congress and vanish across the majority flip.

congresses. Still, completion-regime performance should never be quoted as "prediction."

The 2011-2016 text models, re-implemented faithfully and given modern courtesies (Appendix Table 9), fail under proper scoring: the Gerrish-Blei-style model's raw forecast log loss (0.803) is worse than predicting the base rate—severe miscalibration invisible to the accuracy metrics of the original papers (Figure 3)—and even after temperature repair it trails a member-by-question count table. And the modern stack clears everything within a congress by a wide margin while hitting the same wall as everything else across congresses (Section 5). Table 2 decomposes the signal: each row adds one information source under identical evaluation, separating what member history, metadata, text, and modeling contribute in each regime.

# 2.4 The champion and its certification

The final model is a logistic blend of three two-tower networks. Each tower embeds members in a latent space and amortizes each rollcall's choice parameters from its text—the vote question, the vote description, and the latest pre-vote bill summary, encoded by modern sentence encoders (the three towers vary the encoder and text representation)—anchored by a member-by-question-type empirical rate and calibrated on a temporal internal slice of training data. Development followed a pre-registered ledger; the ablation path

<!-- PAGE BREAK -->

![img-1.jpeg](img-1.jpeg)
Figure 2: Member-by-member geometric mean probability on identical held-out completion-regime votes: this paper's spatial model against the classical model (frozen DW-NOMINATE positions, estimated rollcall parameters). Members above the diagonal fit better under the richer model; the largest gains (labeled) are the literature's canonical hard cases. Blue: Democrats; red: Republicans.

and the full set of negative results (bigger encoders, recency weighting, within-bill context features, per-bucket calibration) are in Appendix D with their diagnosed mechanisms.

On the temporal forecast test—689,351 future votes—the blend reaches 0.324 log loss,  $86.2\%$  accuracy,  $85.1\%$  contested accuracy, APRE 0.48. For certification beyond any possibility of test-set contamination, we are exact about which artifact certifies what. The single-tower predecessor (v1) was frozen on June 12, 2026 (weights hash-pinned, fit through the June 9 data snapshot) and scored 0.331 log loss and  $85.7\%$  accuracy on the 43 rollcalls and 9,451 votes Congress cast June 10–30 (rollcall-cluster bootstrap  $95\%$  interval for the log loss: 0.265–0.402). That interval is wide—43 rollcalls is a contamination check, not a precision instrument—and its value is what it rules out: the development-test numbers are not inflated by adaptive iteration. The ensemble champion (v2) was frozen on July 3, 2026 on the same data snapshot; scored on the same window it ties the predecessor on log loss with one point higher accuracy, and its pristine window (votes after July 3) accumulates going forward. All regime-audit and measurement results in this

paper come from the ensemble; the prospective certificate quoted in the abstract belongs to the predecessor, with the ensemble's ledger running.

# 3 Measuring Members

# 3.1 Positions, validated

The instrument's member representations should first reproduce what we already trust. Fitting the benchmark's one-dimensional spatial model per chamber (with the identification conventions documented in the repository), learned positions correlate with DW-NOMINATE's first dimension at  $r = .98$  in both chambers—and at .84-.91 within party, where the information is (Figure 4). Nothing about DW-NOMINATE entered the estimation; the agreement is two instruments discovering the same object.

# 3.2 Loyalty beyond ideology

Ideology and discipline are conceptually distinct—a centrist who always answers the whip differs from a centrist who defects—but one-dimensional scores conflate them. Using the

<!-- PAGE BREAK -->

![img-2.jpeg](img-2.jpeg)
Figure 3: Calibration on strictly future rollcalls. The 2011-style model's probabilities are badly overconfident—the failure mode that accuracy metrics cannot see and that makes uncalibrated model output unusable as measurement.

instrument, we compute each member's expected behavior on party-unity votes given their position, and define the loyalty residual as actual minus expected unity-vote support. Figure 5 shows the recent-era distribution for every eligible member under the caption's inclusion rule (at least 100 unity votes; territorial delegates excluded), and its two systematic properties. The distribution is tight and asymmetric: most members are within a point or two of their position-predicted loyalty, with a long defector tail and almost no excess-loyalty tail. The pattern is consistent with a loyalty ceiling enforced by party pressure, though part of the asymmetry is mechanical—when expected support is near one, positive residuals are bounded near zero—so the informative reading is the defector tail, not the missing loyal one. The residual is also essentially uncorrelated with ideological extremity, measured as the absolute ideal point ( $\rho = -.05$ ): the measure captures a dimension of behavior that position does not. The labeled extremes—who emerge from the full distribution rather than from selection—are the members journalists and whips would name: Walter Jones as the era's outstanding maverick, then Justin Amash, Collin Peterson, Ileana Ros-Lehtinen. The residual is discipline net of the position variable used in the expected-support model, though it may also absorb preference variation—issue-specific positions, for instance—that the overall position does not capture; with at least 100 unity votes per member, binomial sampling error is at most about five points, several times smaller than the labeled extremes.

<!-- PAGE BREAK -->

![img-3.jpeg](img-3.jpeg)
Figure 4: Validation: positions learned by the benchmark's spatial model (vertical axis) against DW-NOMINATE (horizontal), by chamber. Blue: Democrats; red: Republicans.

![img-4.jpeg](img-4.jpeg)

# 3.3 Issue-specific positions

Issue-specific structure has a distinguished lineage—appropriations votes scale differently from the rest of the agenda (Crespin and Rohde, 2010), and topic-weighted estimation recovers issue variation in courts and Congress (Lauderdale and Clark, 2014)—but coverage has been the constraint. Because every rollcall here carries a policy area from its bill's text metadata, the spatial model can be re-estimated within policy areas, giving each member a position in each of the thirteen policy domains of Table 4—the structure a single dimension is forced to average away. Coverage is broad: under the fifty-vote rule, between 917 and 1,562 members receive a position in each of the twelve legislative areas (nominations, a Senate-only domain, covers 328), and Table 4 reports the full per-topic accounting.

Three systematic facts emerge. First, one dimension is almost sufficient: within-area positions correlate with overall positions at  $\rho = .95 - .99$  (Figure 6). High correlations alone cannot distinguish structured deviations from small random errors; what carries the structure claim is that the deviations recover coherent factions—the same members deviating the same direction across related areas, matching the qualitative record—as documented below and in Table 4. Second, polarization is not uniform across the agenda (Figure 7). The parties stand farthest apart on energy and taxation—the modern eco

<!-- PAGE BREAK -->

nomic core—and closest together on armed forces and international affairs; within-party dispersion is also high in exactly those areas, with armed forces showing the largest Democratic spread and international affairs the largest Republican spread (Table 4): foreign policy is simultaneously the least partisan and among the most internally contested terrain in both parties. Third, the deviations recover known ideological factions from votes alone, systematically rather than anecdotally. Reading down Table 4's largest recent-era deviators: the liberal-direction outliers on armed forces and international affairs are the libertarian Republicans (Paul, Crane, Roy, Sanford), civil-libertarian deviations appear on crime (Brat, Burlison), and hawkish or border-district Democrats deviate conservatively in the same areas. None of these members' texts, speeches, or biographies entered the estimation; their votes alone locate them.

# 3.4 Out-of-character votes (in-sample residuals)

A calibrated model of member behavior defines, vote by vote, what would be surprising—and its largest realized surprises are a dataset no existing measure provides. We are precise about the estimand: the scores here are in-sample residuals—the frozen champion is fit on the member's entire record through mid-2026 and then asked about each historical vote, so a large score means the vote is out-of-character relative to everything the member ever did,

<!-- PAGE BREAK -->

4 Measuring Bills

The same machinery measures the agenda. Every rollcall acquires a discrimination (how strongly it separates the space) and a cutpoint—the position of an indifferent member. Figure 9 plots the 118th House: the member distribution is bimodal and the cutpoint distribution concentrates in the empty center—the signature of an agenda organized to divide the parties—but the policy-area breakdown shows the exceptions in measurable form: areas whose median cutpoint sits inside a party (internal factional fights) and areas whose cutpoints sit at the extremes (consensus votes that separate only the flanks).

Cutpoint locations relative to the chamber’s pivotal actors are the raw material of the agenda-control literature (Richman, 2011; Peress, 2013; Aldrich, Montgomery and Sparks, 2014), and the full-agenda measurement makes the standard quantities immediate. In the 118th House, only 15.0% of the 984 identified cutpoints fall between the floor median (0.48) and the majority-party median (1.77)—the region where a vote separates the majority party’s own median from its right flank—while 69.1% fall between the Democratic median (-3.62) and the floor median: the party-line region where, given the fitted orientation of each rollcall’s discrimination, the two parties’ medians sit on opposite sides of the threshold. That asymmetry—an agenda whose divisions run between the parties rather than through the majority—is negative agenda control rendered as a histogram, computed from every recorded vote of the congress rather than a curated subset.

Text enters the measurement here: the model’s text-to-parameter head is a learned map from legislative language to conflict structure, and its successes and failures delineate what bill content is to a coalition. On final-passage votes, text carries real information—the ablation path in Appendix Table 10 shows rollcall-level text construction contributing some of the largest gains. On amendments, Section 5 shows the opposite, and the contrast is itself the measurement: language predicts coalitions where coalitions form on content, and the places it fails are the places procedure and bargaining, not content, do the organizing.

4.1 One vote, walked through

Before the aggregate exhibits, one rollcall end to end—chosen from the genuine holdout window, so every model quantity below was computed without access to this vote or any later one. On December 20, 2024, the House passed H.R. 10545, the American Relief Act (roll no. 1235), the continuing resolution that averted a Christmas shutdown after the collapse of the leadership’s first package—a storied majority-splitting vote. The realized

not that it was unforeseeable at the time. (The prospective ledger produces the strictly forecast-based version of this measure for every vote cast after the freeze, and will accumulate it going forward.) Figure 8 shows the decisions the model still gets confidently wrong even with full hindsight. These are not model noise; inspected, they are recognizable episodes—high-pressure defections, district-driven crossovers, protest votes—now identifiable systematically rather than anecdotally. We release the full surprise ranking as a research resource: a sampling frame for studying lobbying, leadership pressure, and the votes that end careers.

Two clarifications locate this measure in the literature's lineage. Relative to legislator-specific variance in a one-dimensional model (Lauderdale, 2010), our unpredictability is residual to a far richer specification: behavior must escape text, issue structure, party discipline, and the member's own detailed history before it registers as surprising. And relative to protest voting (Fowler and Lewis, 2026): the Squad's protest pattern is regular—a rich model learns it, which is exactly why those members show ordinary loyalty residuals and no excess presence in our surprise tail, while a one-dimensional model registers them as its worst-fit members (Figure 2). What the classical framework treats as measurement failure is, to a sufficiently expressive model, simply another behavioral signature; what remains in our surprise tail is the genuinely episodic residue.

<!-- PAGE BREAK -->

4 Measuring Bills

The same machinery measures the agenda. Every rollcall acquires a discrimination (how strongly it separates the space) and a cutpoint—the position of an indifferent member. Figure 9 plots the 118th House: the member distribution is bimodal and the cutpoint distribution concentrates in the empty center—the signature of an agenda organized to divide the parties—but the policy-area breakdown shows the exceptions in measurable form: areas whose median cutpoint sits inside a party (internal factional fights) and areas whose cutpoints sit at the extremes (consensus votes that separate only the flanks).

Cutpoint locations relative to the chamber’s pivotal actors are the raw material of the agenda-control literature *(Richman, 2011; Peress, 2013; Aldrich, Montgomery and Sparks, 2014)*, and the full-agenda measurement makes the standard quantities immediate. In the 118th House, only 15.0% of the 984 identified cutpoints fall between the floor median (0.48) and the majority-party median (1.77)—the region where a vote separates the majority party’s own median from its right flank—while 69.1% fall between the Democratic median (-3.62) and the floor median: the party-line region where, given the fitted orientation of each rollcall’s discrimination, the two parties’ medians sit on opposite sides of the threshold. That asymmetry—an agenda whose divisions run between the parties rather than through the majority—is negative agenda control rendered as a histogram, computed from every recorded vote of the congress rather than a curated subset.

Text enters the measurement here: the model’s text-to-parameter head is a learned map from legislative language to conflict structure, and its successes and failures delineate what bill content is to a coalition. On final-passage votes, text carries real information—the ablation path in Appendix Table 10 shows rollcall-level text construction contributing some of the largest gains. On amendments, Section 5 shows the opposite, and the contrast is itself the measurement: language predicts coalitions where coalitions form on content, and the places it fails are the places procedure and bargaining, not content, do the organizing.

On December 20, 2024, the House passed H.R. 10545, the American Relief Act (roll no. 1235), the continuing resolution that averted a Christmas shutdown after the collapse of the leadership’s first package—a storied majority-splitting vote. spatial fit gives it negative discrimination (-1.11) with a cutpoint at 3.44: the far-right flank versus everyone else, exactly the recorded 366-34 coalition (92% yea among the 400 members scored). Table 5 shows the forecast member-by-member for leadership, flank, and cross-pressed members: the model, seeing only pre-vote information, put near-certain yeas on both parties' leaderships and priced the right-flank revolt member by member: its five likeliest defectors in the entire chamber—Brecheen, Roy, Massie, Crane, Biggs—all voted nay. The realized revolt arrives as a sequence of small, correctly-located surprises, not shocks.

# 4.2 Predicting where a bill will cut, from its text

The dashboard's premise—that a bill's language locates it on the scale before anyone votes—can be tested directly, and Table 6 and Figure 10 do so. For every rollcall in the benchmark we take the realized cutpoint and direction (which party's side the yeas fall on) from the spatial fits, hold out the final twenty percent of each congress-chamber's rollcalls, and ask how well features available before the vote predict them: nothing; metadata (question type, sponsor party, bill category); text alone (in classical and modern representations); or text and metadata together. On 3,549 held-out future rollcalls, text plus metadata predicts cutpoint locations with a mean error of 0.64 member-standard-deviations ( $r = 0.55$ , against 0.86 with no features) and calls the coalition's direction correctly  $74.5\%$  of the time (chance:  $55.8\%$ ).

The decomposition is as informative as the headline, and we report the metadata baseline at full strength, which took two rounds of honest reinforcement. A bill's sponsor is directional for passage votes (the yeas fall on the sponsor's side 82 percent of the time)

<!-- PAGE BREAK -->

Table 6: Predicting held-out future rollcalls' cutpoints and coalition directions from pre-vote features. Cutpoint error in member-standard-deviation units, identified rollcalls; direction accuracy on identified rollcalls. Bottom panel: text components separately (TF-IDF pipeline).

|  Features | Cutpoint MAE | Cutpoint r | Direction acc. (%)  |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
|  Constant | 0.856 | 0.00 | 55.8  |
|  Metadata only | 0.657 | 0.48 | 70.8  |
|  Text: TF-IDF | 0.742 | 0.43 | 61.3  |

but a coin flip for amendment votes, where the relevant actor is the amender—frequently the other party attacking the bill. The final metadata model therefore carries both actors' parties (bill sponsor everywhere; amender's party on the roughly three-quarters of amendment votes recoverable from amendment records, where the amender-side rule alone calls direction at 77 percent), sponsor-by-question interactions, question type, bill category, and chamber. So strengthened, metadata alone is formidable: it locates cutpoints nearly as well as anything (MAE = 0.66; where a vote cuts is substantially institutional) and calls direction at  $70.8\%$ . Text still earns its keep on every metric: direction rises to  $74.5\%$  and the correlation of predicted with realized locations from  $r = 0.48$  to 0.55. Among the text's components, the bill summary carries the most directional signal and the vote question the most locational signal (Table 6, bottom panel). The synthesis: institutions pin where a vote cuts, the acting legislator's party supplies a default side, and content is what tells you when the default is wrong—and by how much.

# 4.3 The language of left- and right-recruiting bills

What in the text carries the directional signal? Fitting an interpretable sparse model (TF-IDF terms, final-passage votes, identified rollcalls, alphabetic tokens only) exposes the coefficients directly, and Figure 11 shows the result: a lexicon of coalition recruitment. Bills whose summaries establish programs, authorize assistance, and provide grants and services to workers and employees recruit liberal-yea coalitions; bills about rules, regulations, permits, budgets, statutory revision (sec, code, amends), and injury (the tort-reform lexicon) recruit conservative-yea ones. The map is, recognizably, the administrative state

<!-- PAGE BREAK -->

built versus the administrative state checked—estimated from votes and bill summaries alone. We reached this exhibit through three pre-registered face-validity corrections worth recording, because each is a warning to text-as-data practice: naive fits are dominated first by year tokens (which proxy for who held the majority—agenda composition, not language), then by procedural boilerplate from rules resolutions; only after restricting to substantive passage-vote summaries does bill *language* itself speak.

Because the map from text to conflict structure is an estimated object, it can be queried with bills that do not exist. We release, alongside the paper, an interactive forecaster: paste the text of a hypothetical bill, name a sponsoring party and chamber, and the frozen model returns a predicted cutpoint, party-by-party support, and each current member’s vote probability (Appendix C). The tool exposes the instrument’s boundaries as deliberately as its capabilities: because floor agendas are curated by the majority party, the model has never observed votes on off-agenda bills, and the dashboard flags forecasts whose internal structure reveals extrapolation—for instance, when the predicted coalition contradicts the bill’s own sponsorship. The dashboard is thus both a research tool and a live demonstration of the agenda-selection boundary discussed above.

## 5 Measuring the Limits—and What They Say About Congress

### 5.1 Amendments: deals, not content

Amendment votes carry roughly thirty percent of the instrument’s remaining error (Table 7), and two-thirds of amendment rollcalls lack amendment-specific text—so supplying the amendments’ actual purpose language, parsed from BILLSTATUS records and joined through recorded-vote references, looked like the obvious improvement. (The join filled amendment-side information for 3,421 of the 4,818 previously text-starved amendment rollcalls, of 7,105 total.) Pre-registered, it failed three ways, and the failure is the finding. Corrupted joins, corrected joins, and minimal-footprint text insertions all made prediction *worse* by similar amounts; a placebo rebuild that changed the data file without changing content reproduced the champion exactly, exonerating the pipeline. Mechanism: when a bill’s amendments share the parent bill’s text, the model assigns the series nearly identical parameters—an implicit pooled estimate of a correlated block. Purpose language replaces that pooling with per-amendment, content-driven parameters that predict worse than the bill’s identity does. We state the boundary precisely rather than expansively:

<!-- PAGE BREAK -->

Table 7: Where the instrument's error lives: test log loss by question type, prior single-tower champion versus the final blend.

|  Question type | N | Prior champion LL | Blend LL | Share of loss (%)  |
| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
|  amendment | 114,184 | 0.613 | 0.602 | 30.7  |
|  veto | 382 | 0.530 | 0.506 | 0.1  |
|  conference | 10,989 | 0.452 | 0.412 | 2.0  |
|  cloture | 20,857 | 0.363 | 0.334 | 3.1  |
|  passage | 267,925 | 0.330 | 0.300 | 36.0  |
|  nomination | 15,312 | 0.322 | 0.282 | 1.9  |
|  procedural | 117,873 | 0.267 | 0.249 | 13.1  |
|  other | 68,813 | 0.266 | 0.242 | 7.4  |
|  resolution | 73,016 | 0.196 | 0.170 | 5.6  |

short amendment purpose text adds nothing once bill identity and procedural context are modeled—while actor identity adds a great deal (the amender's party alone moves direction accuracy on amendment votes from a coin flip to 77 percent; Section 4.1). Coalitions on amendments are organized by who is acting on which bill at which procedural moment far more than by what the amendment's text says—a measured boundary of the text-as-data program that corroborates, at the level of individual votes, accounts of amendment politics as coalition management.

# 5.2 Majority control: role, not preference

The 118th Congress column of Table 1 is the deepest lesson. Trained through 2021 and asked about 2023-24—across the House majority flip—the champion posts higher log loss than a count table keyed only on majority status, chamber, and question type (0.643 versus 0.617); pooled member histories are outright poisonous (log loss 0.740), because the member who reliably supported procedural motions as a majority member reliably opposes them in the minority. A synthetic two-world experiment in the appendix isolates the mechanism: where behavior is majority-role-driven, member history anti-transfers exactly as observed; where behavior is a stable trait, it transfers nearly perfectly. Real congressional voting is a blend, and on the procedural votes that fill the calendar, the role component dominates.

Because one transition proves little, we repeat the exercise at every House transition in the text-era window—the 110th, 112th, 116th, and 118th Congresses, where the majority flipped, against the 113th, 115th, 117th, and 119th as placebo transitions (Figure 12). The design separates two costs. Crossing any congress boundary is expensive—congress-keyed

<!-- PAGE BREAK -->

member histories collapse everywhere—but the flip-specific damage is located precisely where the role mechanism predicts: the champion’s procedural-vote log loss averages 1.105 across flips against 0.613 across placebos, while final-passage transfer is essentially flip-invariant (0.447 versus 0.427). Passage coalitions travel; procedural coalitions are creatures of who holds the gavel. The general claim is accordingly narrowed to what the evidence shows: what fails to survive a change in majority control is the procedural-role component of voting behavior—which is, however, a large share of the modern rollcall agenda.

Attempts to repair the model with majority-status features fail for an identifiable reason—within a congress, member history is strictly more informative, so the model never learns to use the features that would survive the flip. Meanwhile the logit on frozen, career-spanning DW-NOMINATE scores nearly matches the deep model’s transfer accuracy (62.0% versus 62.1%).

For measurement practice the implications are direct, and they extend a caution the American political development literature has pressed on other grounds: cross-time comparisons of vote-based scores are entangled with changes in what the chamber votes on *(Bateman et al., 2017)*. Vote-derived scores estimated within one majority configuration partly measure that configuration; comparisons of member scores across a change in control confound preference change with role change; and the durability of career-spanning estimation—long derided as an assumption of implausible preference stability—looks instead like insurance against role contamination. For the benchmark, cross-configuration transfer is the posted challenge: no model we tested, and none we could design under pre-registration, solves it.

## 6 Conclusion

Better prediction has made Congress more measurable: positions that match the classical canon, loyalty separated from ideology, issue positions recovered from votes alone, an agenda mapped bill by bill, and a systematic catalog of the votes no model saw coming. It has also made the limits measurable, and they are not incidental: legislative language organizes some coalitions and not others, and a large share of observed voting behavior belongs to institutional role rather than to the member. Instruments, measures, and their documented boundaries are released together—including a frozen model whose forecasting claims the future itself will continue to audit.

# B Feature Timing and Text Coverage

# C The Interactive Forecaster

The dashboard wraps the frozen champion's MiniLM component tower. (An earlier draft claimed the blend's TF-IDF component could not run on a single pasted bill; that was wrong—frozenidf weights transform new documents fine. The component is omitted as an engineering simplification, justified by its small blend weight of 0.12.) Pasted text is assembled into the training text template and embedded with the training encoder; a synthetic bill record carries the user-specified sponsoring party through the same features used in training; member vote probabilities are computed for the full current roster; and the displayed cutpoint is the position at which predicted support crosses one half, from a logistic fit of predicted votes on the current congress's estimated positions—mirroring the paper's cutpoint estimation on realized votes. The display rule guards both failure modes of that fit: a cutpoint is reported only when the fitted slope exceeds a minimum magnitude and the crossing falls within the observed range of current-member positions; otherwise it is suppressed as unidentified, since a logistic fitted to near-unanimous predictions can cross one half far outside the chamber. Sanity behavior: a commemorative post-office naming yields near-unanimous predicted support and no identified cutpoint; a sponsor-aligned

<!-- PAGE BREAK -->

tax bill yields a party-line forecast with a center-left cutpoint. Off-agenda hypotheticals trigger the extrapolation flag described in the text. The service is a single CPU process suitable for a small container host; deployment configuration accompanies the repository.

# D The Experiment Ledger

Every development experiment was pre-registered in the repository's ledger before its first run, with a hypothesis, a pre-vote-knowability argument for any new feature, and a falsification test. Selection used validation sets only; the three-model final comparison observed the test sets once. The ledger records, with diagnosed mechanisms: the encoder-scale negative (a  $16 \times$  larger text budget does not improve forecasts and worsens long-bill votes); the amendment-text negatives and their placebo control (Section 5); the recency-weighting negative (decayed member rates add estimator noise exceeding any drift signal); the within-bill context negative (features that pass synthetic tests at the information-theoretic optimum destabilize joint training on real data); the per-bucket calibration negative; and the regime-inheritance result (parameters fitted only on the temporal development slice—inside training data—transfer to temporal evaluation and break on interleaved evaluation). Full reproduction instructions accompany the repository, and Table 11 gives the compact audit trail for the experiments this paper relies on most.

|  E1 | within-bill context helps | shuffled-bill placebo | negative (joint)  |
|  E1b | stacked context corrector | permuted-bill weights → 0 | null  |
|  E2 | recency-weighted rates help | τ → ∞ recovers flat | negative  |
|  E3 | per-bucket calibration helps | val only | negative  |
